coredata.dc:title: Preparation of CDs-TiO2/SiCf materials by cladding carbonization method and the degradation of enrofloxacin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuo
Liu, Yanping
Guo, Huahua
Niu, Yanli
Gao, Tian
Wang, Yuerong
Gao, Jianfeng
Bai, Peikang
coredata.dc:description: The material CDs-TiO2/SiCf (TS) comprising graphite-phase carbon dots was generated in the TiO2/SiCf system using the encapsulated carbonization method. The prepared materials were characterized using X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectroscopy (Raman), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which revealed that SiCf, CDs, and TiO2 are strongly coupled to form a Z-type heterojunction. The UV diffuse reflectance spectra displayed that the TS was red-shifted, with a shorter bandgap (1.40 eV) and a wider light absorption range (200–800 nm) than SiCf and TiO2, indicating a superior photocatalytic performance. The developed heat-resistant materials are intended to be used as head-cone components for stealthy aircraft with visible light absorption. Photocatalytic degradation studies exhibited that TS materials degraded enrofloxacin to an extent of 83.1 % and the TOC removal efficiency was 51.3 %, at the dosage of 0.07 mg·L−1 and the concentration of 10 mg·L−1. Furthermore, TS demonstrated exceptional reproducibility and stability in cycle regeneration experiments. Quenching tests and electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (ESR) revealed that e−, and ·O2 − are the primary active substances in the photocatalytic degradation of enrofloxacin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enrofloxacin
Photocatalytic degradation
Silicon carbide fibers
Visible hotocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. XRD spectra of TS, TiO2, and SiCf; b. Raman plot of TS.
a. BET analysis of TiO2 and SiCf; b. BET analysis of TS.
Elemental mapping images of a C; b N; c Si and d Ti; e. Surface profiles of C, O, Si and Ti. f. EDS analysis spectrum of TS (atomic percentage table (inset)).
a SEM, b TEM, and c HRTEM related to the SiCf; d SEM, e TEM, and f HRTEM related to the TiO2; g SEM, h TEM, and i HRTEM related to the TS
a. XPS measurement spectra of TS; b. C 1s XPS spectra of SiCf and TS; c. O 1s XPS spectra of TiO2 and TS; d. Si 2p XPS spectra of SiCf and TS; e. Ti 2p XPS spectra of TiO2 and TS.

UV-Vis absorption spectra of SiCf and (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (inset); b. UV-Vis absorption spectra of TiO2 (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (inset); c. UV-Vis absorption spectra of TS and (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (inset); d. SiCf′ s valence band XPS; e. Valence band XPS of TiO2.
Fluorescence spectra of SiCf, TiO2 and TS; b. electrochemical impedance spectra of SiCf, TiO2 and TS.
SiCf, TiO2 and TS degradation of enrofloxacin in visible light; b. Kinetic modeling of the reaction (EN = 10 mg L−1).
a. Effect of a. catalyst dosage, and b. initial EN concentration; c. initial pH on the EN removal rate in the presence of TS.
TOC removal efficiency by TS under different light times.
a. Schematic diagram of four-cycle regeneration of Enrofloxacin degradation by TS photocatalytic system; b. TS fluorescence at 420 nm before and after cycling.

a. Effects of different quenchers on the degradation of EN by TS; b. DMPO-·O2 − and c. DMPO-·OH spin-trapping ESR spectra in aqueous solution in the presence of samples under simulated visible-light irradiation
The proposed mechanism of TS under simulated visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Preparation of CDs-TiO2/SiCf materials by cladding carbonization method and the degradation of enrofloxacin

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The material CDs-TiO2/SiCf (TS) comprising graphite-phase carbon dots was generated in the TiO2/SiCf system using the encapsulated carbonization method. The prepared materials were characterized using X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectroscopy (Raman), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which revealed that SiCf, CDs, and TiO2 are strongly coupled to form a Z-type heterojunction. The UV diffuse reflectance spectra displayed that the TS was red-shifted, with a shorter bandgap (1.40 eV) and a wider light absorption range (200–800 nm) than SiCf and TiO2, indicating a superior photocatalytic performance. The developed heat-resistant materials are intended to be used as head-cone components for stealthy aircraft with visible light absorption. Photocatalytic degradation studies exhibited that TS materials degraded enrofloxacin to an extent of 83.1 % and the TOC removal efficiency was 51.3 %, at the dosage of 0.07 mg·L−1 and the concentration of 10 mg·L−1. Furthermore, TS demonstrated exceptional reproducibility and stability in cycle regeneration experiments. Quenching tests and electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (ESR) revealed that e−, and ·O2 − are the primary active substances in the photocatalytic degradation of enrofloxacin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry and agriculture as well as the rapid population growth, a large number of organic pollutants have been frequently detected in wastewater in recent decades, posing a serious threat to aquatic organisms. While dyes and antibiotics are difficult to be completely degraded due to their stable molecular structure. Enrofloxacin, a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is widely used in the treatment of animal infections, but it may cause damage to human DNA [1]. The risk assessment indicated that the risk of enrofloxacin in the aqueous environment is higher compared to other antibiotics [2]. Therefore, the selection of suitable catalytic materials and degradation methods applied for the degradation of enrofloxacin in aqueous solution has become an urgent task.

Silicon carbide (SiC), a promising non-oxide ceramic material, is an appealing material for photocatalysts due to its ideal bandgap structure, excellent thermal and chemical stability, and outstanding semiconducting material properties [3–5]. After carbon fiber, SiCf (silicon carbide fiber) is a new high-performance fiber that exhibits significant application importance in aerospace, defense, and military industries due to its oxidation resistance, high-temperature resistance, and chemical corrosion resistance and belongs to the national strategic emerging material. The stealth modification of aircraft, next-generation weaponized equipment, and the design of high-temperature-resistant parts for high-speed aircraft have employed SiCf owing to its outstanding wave absorption and high-temperature resistance [6–8]. In contrast to TiO2, a conventional semiconductor material, silicon carbide exhibits lower photocatalytic activity because of its narrow absorption spectrum, ease compounding of photogenerated carriers, and low photon quantum efficiency. Therefore, numerous researchers have proposed strategies for improving the material’s photocatalytic efficiency. One method is the formation of heterojunctions through the coupling of SiC with other semiconductors exhibiting distinct energy band structures. SiC develops a unique nanostructure due to the modification of its morphology. Wang et al. developed photocatalytic materials with metal-free core–shell β-SiC@PPCN heterojunctions. PPCN’s excellent stability and compatibility with the β-SiC bandgap protect the core, while the β-SiC@PPCN heterojunction maximizes photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency at a specific temperature, resulting in a material with a high rate of photothermal catalytic hydrogen production [9]. Conversely, materials exhibiting excellent photocatalytic performance can be derived by designing the effects introduced by the nanoscale’s different dimensions into the preparation scheme of silicon carbide materials, thereby causing the silicon carbide to form a unique nanostructure, including carbon quantum dots, nanoparticles, and hollow spheres, etc. For instance, Soheila Asadzadeh-Khaneghah et al. employed a facile method to prepare metal-free ternary g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposites and the absorbance was significantly enhanced in the visible region by increasing the SiC dosage compared to the single C3N4, as well as due to the enhanced light trapping ability of the C-Dots [10]. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of the materials was improved after C-Dots were deposited on CN-SiC (40 %) nanocomposites, leading to an increase in absorbance in the visible region. Titanium dioxide is a common semiconductor material composited with CDs or excellent photocatalytic performance. Wang et al. produced Au/CQDs-TiO2 NRs ternary photocatalysts by utilizing a continuous ionic layer adsorption reaction and a simple hydrothermal method [11]. The coupling of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with titanium dioxide improves light harvesting while also facilitating charge separation and transfer, and the use of gold nanoparticles as localized surface plasmon resonance sensitizers (LSPRs) accelerates electron and hole dissociation and improves photocatalytic activity in both the UV and visible light ranges. Jiang et al. immobilised nitrogen-carbon co-doped titanium dioxide nanocatalysts (NCD-TiO2) in poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) matrix and formed PVA/NCDTiO2 films by solution casting and heat treatment, which showed a degradation rate of 42 % for levofloxacin after 2 h [12]. Hiromi Yamashita et al. calcined TiC-SiC precursor nanoparticles obtained by carbon-dioxide reduction method to prepare TiO2 photocatalyst deposited on SiC (TiO2-SiC) and successfully used it for photocatalytic degradation of diluted 2-propanol in water under UV irradiation [13], and it was observed that the hydrophobicity of the SiC surface was related to the highly efficient photocatalytic reaction activity of the TiO2-SiC photocatalyst SiCf emiconductor materials can be utilized to manufacture aircraft engine casings, stealth cruise missile head cones, rocket engine casings, and other aerospace and military applications due to their high temperature and oxidation resistance and capability to absorb visible light [14–16].

In this work, a one-step simple-to-operate cladding carbonization method was utilized to synthesize CDs-TiO2/SiCf (TS), a material incorporating graphene carbon dots. However, TiO2 can be loaded on SiCf to develop heterojunctions to improve photocatalytic performance regardless of its narrow forbidden band. CDs can easily capture photogenerated electrons, transfer them to catalysts on the surface, and accelerate charge transfer, thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the materials. The prepared composite TS facilitates electron diffusion and transfer while inhibiting photogenerated carriers complexation, leading to a photocatalyst TS with good catalytic performance because of the conductivity of carbon material and the stability of silicon carbide, as well as the heterojunction formed by the tight bonding between CDs, SiCf, and TiO2. In this project, the coating carbonization method is used to obtain the material’s TS, which is characterized and analyzed by a variety of analytical methods; The fluoroquinolone typical drug enrofloxacin removal rate is used to characterize the material’s photocatalytic activity, which provides an innovative approach for stealthy aircraft manufacturing, new-generation weaponized equipment design, and so on.

Silicon carbide fibers (SiCf), pure analysis, was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Fangzheng Reagent Factory. Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti), Glacial acetic acid (C2H4O2), Formic acid (HCOOH), Anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O), pure analysis, was acquired from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Enrofloxacin (C19H22FN3O3), pure analysis, was supplied by Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Company. Homemade deionized water was obtained from the YN-ZD-Z-10 water purification system. The above-mentioned chemical reagents were utilized directly.

The crystal structure of TS was investigated using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Ultima IV) with a 2θ = 10–80° range. The Raman spectrum of TS was recorded utilizing a Renishaw in Via Raman spectrometer under a 532 nm He-Ne laser source. The morphology of the material TS was examined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS MERLIN Compact), transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), and an X-ray energy spectrometer (EDS). The fluorescence characteristics of the material TS were determined by employing a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600) with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The TS photocurrent curves were recorded using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E). The light absorbance range of the samples was measured using a UV-2300 spectrophotometer with BaSO4 as the reference material. The elemental composition of TS and its chemical valence were identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency of the samples was determined using a total organic carbon analyser (Thermo Scientific Vario TOC, USA). Active substances produced during the degradation of enrofloxacin were analyzed using electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (ESR (Sigma A300-10/12)). The specific surface area and pore size distribution of TS were investigated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET).

The TS synthesis procedure is depicted in Fig. 1 . The gel precursor was prepared using the sol–gel method [17]; Solution A contained anhydrous ethanol, glacial acetic acid, and tetrabutyl titanate; Solution B comprised of silicon carbide, glacial acetic acid, formic acid, and the appropriate amount of distilled water (n(Si):n(Ti) = 1:1). Then the mixture of solutions A and B was stirred and placed in a water bath at 60 °C for 2 h. Afterward, the gel precursor was obtained by aging it for a full day at room temperature. The crucible should be filled with the gel precursor, followed by anhydrous ethanol as a flammable agent; Continue adding anhydrous ethanol until the gel does not exceed its capacity to be burned after combustion. This process, referred to as the “carbonization of the propagation,” can be repeated by adding anhydrous ethanol and ignition as described above. TS composites of CDs-SiCf@TiO2 can be synthesized in one one-step using this simple preparation method, which produces CDs through combustion.

Enrofloxacin, a typical antibiotic belonging to the fluoroquinolone group, was removed at rate that indicated the fluorocatalytic activity of material TS. The TS was dispersed into 100 mL of enrofloxacin solution with varied concentrations, then the solution was held in the dark for 60 min to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium; the solution was placed under a 250 W metal halide lamp (Shanghai Yaming Lighting Appliance Manufacturing Co, China) as a simulated visible light source for photocatalytic experiments and photographed at regular intervals; and samples were separated and centrifuged using a 0.22 μm polythersulfone membrane filter to remove the catalyst, and the absorbance value of the enrofloxacin solution at 271 nm was measured using a UV–visible spectrometer. The extent of undegradation of enrofloxacin was afterward estimated using the degradation residual percentage η as follows:
(1)
η = C /
C 0
× 100 % = A /
A 0
× 100 %

Furthermore, various UV photocatalytic activity curves that visually represent the catalytic performance of the catalysts can be produced by repeating the above operation and photodegrading different materials.

The XRD spectra of TS, TiO2, and SiCf are shown in Fig. 2 a. The TS plots illustrate the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.28°, 37.80°, 48.05°, 53.89°, 62.69°, 68.76°, and 75.03° (JCSPD nos: 21–1272) corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (204), (220), and (215) crystallographic planes of anatase TiO2 [18]. In addition to this, the crystal planes (111), (211), (220), (311), and (222) crystal planes of SiCf are matched at 2θ = 36.95°, 41.42°, 60.07°, 71.81°, and 75.52° [19]. Additionally, a distinctive broad peak corresponding to grapheneis exhibited at 25°. TS composites are demonstrated to be formed by the combination of pure TiO2 and pure SiCf while the CDs in TS exhibit a graphene structure.

The Raman spectral analysis results of the TS material are depicted in Fig. 2b. The Raman peaks observed at 152 cm−1, 392 cm−1, 509 cm−1, and 629 cm−1 correspond to the typical anatase TiO2 [20], while the symbolic Raman peaks attributed to the G and D bands of graphene are evident around 1590 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1, respectively, confirming the presence of the CDs with graphene structure in the material in conjunction with abovementioned XRD results. The Raman peak of SiCf corresponding to the absorption band is observed at 778 cm−1.

The results of N2 adsorption/resolution analysis for SiCf, TiO2, and TS are illustrated in Fig. 3 . Among these, the isotherm of SiCf is classified as type II, indicating the presence of nonporous or macroporous adsorption. Both TiO2 and TS exhibit type IV isotherms however, TiO2 exhibitsa hysteresis loop of H1-type while TS has a H2-type hysteresis loop. It is clear from the inset that TiO2 exhibits a considerable amount of macropores and mesopores with the pore diameter in the range of 4–200 nm, while TS contains a higher number of mesopores and macropores, with the pore diameter in the range of 2–8 nm, indicating that it contains abundant mesopores. Owing to the high pore diameter of the mesoporous structure and the effective enlargement of the interface area of the catalyst, the light can easily penetrate the material thereby effectively enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the material. The specific surface area of TS is 88.44 m2·g−1, which is significantly larger than that of 0.17 m2·g−1 for SiCf and 7.34 m2·g−1 for TiO2, attributed to the large pore volume and high specific surface area of TS. The large pore volume and high specific surface area provide TS with a greater number of active reaction sites, thereby effectively improving the photocatalytic efficiency of TS [21,22].

The X-ray energy spectrum (EDS) analysis of the material TS and its surface energy spectrum are depicted in Fig. 4 . As illustrated in Fig. 4(a–d) TS is composed of Ti, O, Si, and C with uniform distribution in each component. Fig. 4f and the atomic percentage table in the inset both demonstrate that TS exhibits an atomic percentage of 1.0:0.26:0.33:0.15 (Ti:O:Si:C).

Fig. 5 shows the results of morphology and microstructure analysis of TS. SiCf exhibits a rod-like structure with a length of 10–50 µm and a diameter of 200–500 nm as demonstrated in Fig. 5(a–c), with the crystal facet spacing of 0.250 nm at the (110) face. The morphology and microstructural analysis spectra of TiO2 exhibiting irregular crystalline particles of 0.1–0.2 μm, with uniform orientation, and the crystal facet spacing of 0.358 nm (110) are depicted in Fig. 5(d–f). It is evident from Fig. 5g, that the surface of SiCf is densely coated with the irregular particles of TiO2. The formation of a heterojunction can be seen from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy results in Fig. 5(h–i), which demonstrates the close proximity of the two semiconductors, TiO2 and SiCf, to form heterojunction. The agglomerated structure exhibits mesopores produced by the TS composites because of the organic components' volatilization during the heat treatment process. It exhibits good absorption properties towards both light and gaseous compounds. Ionic thermal diffusion produces chemical bonding interfaces, ensuring smooth charge transfer [23,24].

The in situ XPS measurement spectra (a) and high-resolution XPS elemental spectra (b-e) of TS, TiO2, and SiCf are displayed in Fig. 6 . C, Ti, O, and S are the predominant elements in TS, as demonstrated in Fig. 6a. The corresponding binding energies of C 1s for the SiC, CC, and CO bonds are 283 eV, 284.8 eV,and 288.6 eV in SiCf and TS, respectively (Fig. 6b), while the peak at 286.5 eV in SiCf is ascribed to the CO2 adsorption on the sample surface. The peaks at 530 eV and 531.7 eV in the spectrogram of O 1s illustrated in Fig. 6c are attributed to TiO and CO bonds respectively, while the peak at 532.6 eV in TS is produced due to the adsorption of oxygen atoms on the sample surface. As demonstrated in Fig. 6d, the peaks at 101 eV and 103.2 eV are attributed to Si 2p3/2 and SiO bonds respectively. Fig. 6e demonstrates that the binding energies of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 are 463.9 eV and 458.3 eV respectively, whereas the Ti 2p1/2 and the Ti 2p3/2 binding energies for TS are 464.6 eV and 458.7 eV respectively, and the binding energy of elemental Ti in the material TS undergoes a positive shift toward the high binding energy region, i.e., electron efflux. Since TiO2 and SiCf are n-type and p-type semiconductors, respectively, and the Fermi energy level of TiO2 is higher than that of SiCf, the electrons of TiO2 in TS migrate toward SiCf, the electron density of the outer layer of Ti ions changes, and the binding energy of the Ti 2p state increases. This further indicates that TiO2 has been sintered on the surface of SiCf and strongly interacts with SiCf [25].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra and the curves of (αhν)1/2 vs. photon energy (inset) of TS, TiO2, and SiCf are shown in Fig. 7 (a–c) SiCf has a good absorption edge extending to the visible region, i.e., 200–412 nm as demonstrated in the figure; TiO2 has an intrinsic absorption edge band of 406 nm, indicating that it only absorbs UV light and has a narrow absorption bandgap; however, the TS material in contrast exhibits a wider absorption band (400–800 nm) than the previous two, i.e., the absorption ranges of TS for pure TiO2 and SiCf are red-shifted compared to those of TiO2, and the absorption capacity is narrower than that of TiO2 and SiCf. In comparison to pure TiO2 and pure SiCf, the absorption range of TS for pure SiCf and pure TiO2 is red-shifted and enhanced. The primary reasonfor the observed redshift is that CDs exhibit specific absorption for 400–500 nm light waves secondly charge transfer, strong binding, and interaction forces between CDs, TiO2, and SiCf, may facilitate charge transfer, thereby broadening its absorption to the visible light region [24]. As demonstrated by XPS nalysis. The band gap of TiO2 is 3.13 eV while that of SiCf is 2.69 eV at n = 2. The plot of (αhν)1/2 versus the photon energy demonstrates thatthe Eg of TS is 1.40 eV. Fig. 7d and e illustrate the precise orientations of the VB maxima (VBM) of SiCf and TiO2 respectively. TiO2 exhibits a VBM of 1.40 eV. The maximum values of VB for SiCf and TiO2 are known to be around 1.85 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively. The conduction band energies of SiCf and TiO2 as determined through calculations are −0.84 eV and −0.58 eV, respectively.

The photoluminescence spectra of TS, TiO2, and SiCf. are illustrated in Fig. 8 a. At an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, the emission peaks of TiO2, SiCf, and TS are similar, but the fluorescence intensity decreases in a particular order such that TiO2 demonstrates the most pronounced fluorescence peaks owing to its high electron-hole complexation rate, while TS displays the weakest fluorescence peaks due to the electron absorption by the CDs in TS which reduces the electron-hole complexation rate and the formation of heterojunctions between the CDs, TiO2 and SiCf, leading to the weakest fluorescence intensity of TS [24], which favors the efficient separation of photogenerated charges.

The spectra for the electrochemical impedance study are presented in Fig. 8b. It is evident. From the figure, that the TS exhibits a smaller arc radius than that of other pure substances, whichsignifies an enhanced higher carrier migration efficiency and consequently the fast interfacial charge transfer. It shows that the TS composites exhibit good visible light photocatalytic performance with close contact between CDs, SiCf, and TiO2 [26].

The results of the visible light irradiation simulation to determine the removal rate of enrofloxacin solution by TS, SiCf, and TiO2 are depicted in Fig. 9 . It is clear from Fig. 9a, that enrofloxacin was degraded extremely inefficiently by SiCf and TiO2 with a degradation rate of 0 % and 23 % after 180 min of light exposure. However, TS exhibited an 83.1 % degradation rate on enrofloxacin. The calculated reaction kinetics of the catalyst as per the proposed first-order model and Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation for the photocatalytic reaction are depicted in Fig. 9b.

The results for the effects of the dosage of catalyst TS, the initial concentration of enrofloxacin solution, and the pH of the aqueous solution on the enrofloxacin removal rate are shown in Fig. 10 a, b, and c, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 10a, at a catalyst TS dosage of 0.07 g·L−1, the degradation rate of enrofloxacin was 83.1 % which can be attributed to the increased number of reaction sites facilitating the generation of more free radicals. However, the degradation rate of enrofloxacin decreased to 69.0 % when the TS dosage was higher than 0.07 g·L−1, the degradation rate of enrofloxacin decreased to 69.0 %, which can be attributed to the fact that an excess amount of the catalyst impedes the light scattering leading to the reduction of photons and subsequently a decrease in photocatalytic activity [27]. Therefore, 0.07 g·L−1 was the optimal TS catalyst dosage. As demonstrated in Fig. 10b, the catalytic performance of enrofloxacin was affected by the initial substrate concentration. At lower concentrations, the ·O2 − radicals were in close proximity to enrofloxacin which accelerated its degradation. Additionally, a significant decrease in the adsorption of light to ground enrofloxacin will occur with an increase in the initial antibiotic concentration leading to a substantial reduction in degradation [28]. The results of the effect of pH on the removal rate of enrofloxacin are illustrated in Fig. 10c with an initial enrofloxacin concentration of 10 mg·L−1 and a catalyst TS amount of 0.07 g·L−1. The results demonstrate that the rate of enrofloxacin removal is maximum at pH = 3, and it decreases with an increase in the pH of the solution. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of TS and the improved removal rate of enrofloxacin may be attributed to the favorable conditions under acidic conditions for the interaction of dissolved oxygen and excited electrons favorable conditions under acidic conditions which produces highly active oxide species ·OH.

In order to further investigate the TOC removal efficiency of TS, the TOC removal efficiency of TS was analysed at different light times. As shown in Fig. 11 , the TOC removal efficiency of TS was 51.3 % when the light time was 180 min. With the increase of reaction time, a large amount of ·OH attacked the enrofloxacin molecules, and the enrofloxacin molecules and the macromolecules produced in the reaction were continuously oxidised to open the ring, generating small molecules, which were ultimately mineralised into H2O, CO2, and so on.

The characterization of the cyclic regeneration performance of TS is illustrated in Fig. 12 . The potential reason for the marginal decrease in enrofloxacin degradation following five cycles of TS photocatalysis through the recycling process is the catalyst loss, which substantiates the reusability of the TS photocatalyst. The similarity in fluorescence of the recycled and fresh samples (Fig. 12b) demonstrated that the surface of TS was not damaged by the recycling experiments, thereby indicating its good stability.

Fig. 13 a shows the results of the main active substance detection experiments. When ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4, 0.1 mM), a trapping agent for h+, benzoquinone (BQ, 0.1 mM), a trapping agent for ·O2 −, and K2CrO4, a trapping agent for e− were added, the photocatalytic efficiency was significantly reduced in comparison to the degradation effect of the original proving that e−, and ·O2 − were the major active substances in the degradation process however the addition of ·OH, a trapping agent for ·OH, DMSO (0.1 mM) to the aqueous solution displayed little effect on the photocatalytic effect of TS, both the main active substances did not contain ·OH. ESR was utilized to further validate the presence of the active substances and the corresponding results are depicted in Fig. 13(b–c). Under only light conditions DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH was produced, reconfirming that ·O2 − and OH were the main active substances in the photocatalytic reaction process.

The potential photocatalytic degradation mechanism of TS is illustrated in Fig. 14 . The CB and VB boundary potentials of SiCf are −0.84 eV and +1.85 eV respectively, determined by the calculations above, while the corresponding CB and VB boundary potentials of TiO2 are −0.58 eV and +2.55 eV, respectively. Both SiCf and TiO2 are excited to produce electrons and holes under simulated solar irradiation. The hotogenerated electrons from the CB of TiO2 can be transmitted to the VB of SiCf through graphene phase carbon and recombined at the formed heterojunction interface. Consequently, the electrons accumulated on the CB of SiCf and reacted with O2 to generate ·O2 − radicals, because the CB position of SiCf was more negative (∼−0.33 eV) than the potential of O2/·O2 −. In addition, adsorbed O2 can form H2O2 by reacting with water molecules and electrons. The resulting H2O2 and ·O2 − can subsequently be converted to hydroxyl radicals (·OH). H2O reacted with the holes produced by the VB of TiO2 to produce ·OH radicals. The VB position of TiO2 is more positive than the standard redox potential OH−/∙OH (1.99 eV vs. NHE) and the reactive species, h+, ·O2 −, e−, and ·OH, produced can further efficiently degrade the target pollutants into smaller intermediates or directly into final products (CO2 and H2O). The transfer process is significantly influenced by the transport function of graphene phase carbon, while the formation of Z-type heterojunction can accelerate the charge separation consequently resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity [29].

In summary, CDs-SiCf/TiO2 (TS), a photocatalytic material exhibiting a visible light response, was successfully synthesized in a single step through a simple and feasible coating carbonization method. The synthesized TS is composed of CDs with a graphene structure, as determined by XRD, Raman, and EDS. The synthesized TS exhibited a Z-type heterojunction with the close bonding of SiCf, CDs, and TiO2. Moreover, pH degradation of enrofloxacin was more facilitated under acidic conditions. The red shift of TS was confirmed by the UV-diffused reflectance spectra. In comparison to SiCf and TiO2, TS exhibited a narrower band gap (1.40 eV), a broader absorption range (200–800 nm), and a greater utilization of visible light, which was conducive to the improvement of the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. Quenching experiments and ESR results showed that e− and ·O2 − were the major active species. In addition, the TS catalyst had good reproducible utility and stability after five cycle experiments. The increased carbon content in the material TS can protect SiCf from decarburization thereby increasing its resistance to high temperature, rendering TS suitable for the development and production of innovative weaponry and high-temperature-resistant components of high-speed aircraft, which could lead to potential applications for the material in the military industry, aerospace, and other sectors.

Shuo Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yanping Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Huahua Guo: Investigation. Yanli Niu: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Tian Gao: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yuerong Wang: Data curation. Jianfeng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Peikang Bai: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U22A20192) and Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Carbon Electrode Materials (202104010910019).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: ShuoZhang is a graduate student majoring in chemistry in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at North Central University. She received her Bachelor of Engineering degree in Applied Chemistry from Putian University. Her research interests focus on the preparation of functional materials and the application of photocatalysts in antibiotic solution removal.
